Infra & Chips.titleZh: Nvidia Delays Kyber NVL144 Rack to 2028 - Let's Data Science
Infra & Chips.content: [Skip to content](#main-content) [LDS Let's Data ScienceLEARN • BUILD • STAY AHEAD](/) [Sign in](/login?redirect=%2Fnews%2Fnvidia-delays-kyber-nvl144-rack-to-2028-294d2182) Infrastructurenvidiakyberrubin ultrapcb midplane # Nvidia Delays Kyber NVL144 Rack to 2028 ||By [LDS Team](/editorial-standards) 7.0 Relevance Score For practitioners, a delayed rack architecture narrows validated scale-up options for largest-model training, affecting procurement and capacity planning for hyperscalers and training clusters. According to research firm SemiAnalysis, the **Kyber NVL144** rack has been delayed to 2028 because a multi-layer PCB midplane is "challenging from a manufacturability standpoint" (reported by CNBC and SemiAnalysis). SemiAnalysis also reported that the interim NVL72x2 stopgap was abandoned after cloud customers pushed back on operational complexity, leaving no proven route to scale **Vera Rubin Ultra** systems in 2027 (reported by The Next Web and Tom's Hardware). Bloomberg- and Yahoo-cited market reports show Asian PCB and supplier stocks fell on the news, with individual suppliers cited as dropping double digits (reported by Yahoo Finance and Bloomberg). ### Editorial analysis This delay matters because rack-level design problems propagate into procurement, deployment timelines, and model-capacity planning for teams running large-scale training workloads. When a vendor-level scale-up path is uncertain, cloud providers and large labs re-evaluate cluster buildouts, spare-part inventories, and deployment automation assumptions. ### What happened According to research firm **SemiAnalysis** (reported by CNBC and Tom's Hardware), Nvidia's **Kyber NVL144** rack architecture has been pushed into 2028 because the centralized circuit board that ties vertical compute trays together, described as the **PCB midplane**, "remains challenging from a manufacturability standpoint." CNBC reproduces SemiAnalysis' phrasing: "Kyber NVL144 rack architecture has been delayed to 2028 as the PCB midplane remains challenging from a manufacturability standpoint." SemiAnalysis also reported that the proposed interim approach, the NVL72x2 back-to-back rack architecture, was dropped after cloud providers pushed back on its operational complexity (reported by The Next Web and Tom's Hardware). SemiAnalysis further flagged that the larger NVL576 system, which would link eight racks over optical connections, is likely delayed or limited to small volumes (reported by CNBC and The Next Web). ### Reported supply-market effects Bloomberg and Yahoo Finance report that Asian PCB and supplier equities slid after the SemiAnalysis disclosure; Yahoo Finance cited specific moves including double-digit drops for several board suppliers (reported by Yahoo Finance citing Bloomberg). Tech press coverage (Techzine, The Next Web) notes that Nvidia's current Rubin systems remain in production and that Rubin shipments to major cloud partners are ongoing this year (reported by The Next Web and Techzine). ### Editorial analysis - technical context The public accounts single out the midplane as the failure point. Techzine reports the midplane is an extremely high-complexity board (they describe it as having **78 layers**) and that such multilayer, high-density boards can suffer low yields and defect sensitivity during scale production. In analogous datacenter hardware efforts, high-layer-count boards and rigid orthogonal backplanes have historically elevated manufacturer qualification time and yield risk, increasing lead times even when the upstream silicon is ready. ### Editorial analysis - implications for engineering and operations Teams planning capacity for 2027-2028 should treat a single-board manufacturability failure as a systemic risk to rack-delivered density. Industry experience shows that when a single mechanical/electrical subsystem constrains volume, organizations often (a) accept lower-density interim designs, (b) shift workloads to existing-generation hardwar...

Infra & Chips.title: Why legacy networks are holding back AI at scale - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Why legacy networks are holding back AI at scale - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.content: * [IT Infrastructure](/it-infrastructure) # Why legacy networks are holding back AI at scale AI’s promise stalls as outdated networks falter. Can enterprises modernize to unlock its full potential? July 6, 2026 5 Min Read SOURCE: TATA COMMUNICATIONS Sponsored by Tata Communications Many enterprise AI initiatives are struggling to move beyond pilots into production. Despite significant investment, only [5% of organizations feel ready to deploy AI at scale](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai). Take a global bank, for example. It spends 18 months developing an AI-driven fraud detection model. In testing it performs well – identifying patterns beyond human capability and showing clear potential to cut losses.  Then it moves into production. To score a transaction at the moment of authorization, each request makes a round trip between the bank’s data center and a cloud-hosted model several regions away, often over the public internet. In isolation, that path adds only tens of milliseconds. Under production load, tail latency balloons – and the authorization window is fixed. When the score arrives too late, the bank has two options: hold the customer’s transaction and degrade the experience, or approve it without the model and forfeit the protection it just spent 18 months building. The model delivers. The path to it doesn’t. Related:[EPB gets grant for quantum workforce development](/it-infrastructure/epb-gets-grant-for-quantum-workforce-development) “If the tech stack is weak, AI is going to stay in pilot mode,” says Sham Chatterjee, Former CIO. “Once you move into production, that’s when the issues start to show. If organizations don’t consider the bandwidth and the latency of AI now, it’s going to catch up with them sooner rather than later.” ## Why the network breaks The reason for this is structural. Enterprise networks were built for a different era. One defined by predictable, human-driven traffic. AI introduces fundamentally new demands. Training workloads require ultra-fast, low-loss connectivity between data centers. In distributed GPU environments, even small delays can disrupt synchronization. Inference also introduces a different kind of pressure. As AI systems move into production, traffic shifts from north-south flows where users access applications, to east-west communication between systems, models and agents.  “Today’s network is built for humans. Tomorrow’s network will be built for agents and humans,” states Rajarshi Purkayastha, Vice President at Tata Communications. “Agents will interact with other agents at machine speed, not human speed. Ten seconds is an eternity for a machine, so the infrastructure and the network have to be designed very differently for that world.”  “The ability to move workloads dynamically and automate troubleshooting is becoming incredibly important,” says Bill Burns, Industry Expert. “I’d love the ability to move workloads instantaneously without human intervention.” Legacy architectures, reliant on best-effort internet routing and fragmented cloud connectivity, struggle to keep up. In AI environments, even small increases in tail latency can stall GPUs , where expensive compute resources sit idle while waiting for delayed packets. The network becomes even more critical in sectors where latency, uptime and compliance are non-negotiable. ## Incremental upgrades are falling short Many organizations respond by adding bandwidth or layering tools onto existing infrastructure. Can enterprises modernize to unlock its full potential? July 6, 2026 5 Min Read SOURCE: TATA COMMUNICATIONS Sponsored by Tata Communications Many enterprise AI initiatives are struggling to move beyond pilots into production. Despite significant investment, only [5% of organizations feel ready to deploy AI at scale](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai). Take a global bank, for example. It spends 18 months developing an AI-driven fraud detection model. In testing it performs well – identifying patterns beyond human capability and showing clear potential to cut losses.  Then it moves into production. To score a transaction at the moment of authorization, each request makes a round trip between the bank’s data center and a cloud-hosted model several regions away, often over the public internet. In isolation, that path adds only tens of milliseconds. Under production load, tail latency balloons – and the authorization window is fixed. When the score arrives too late, the bank has two options: hold the customer’s transaction and degrade the experience, or approve it without the model and forfeit the protection it just spent 18 months building. The model delivers. The path to it doesn’t. The lease is expected to generate about $19 billion of contracted revenue over the initial lease term, according to TeraWulf. "The Anthropic lease validates our strategy and establishes a long-duration revenue stream with one of the world's leading AI companies," TeraWulf CEO Paul Prager said in a statement. Prager added that the lease deal with the [AI firm behind Claude](https://www.businessinsider.com/anthropic-copyright-infringement-claude-code-2026-4) "creates a framework for future expansion, and demonstrates the value of our ability to source power, develop infrastructure, and secure long-term customer commitments." Infra & Chips.content: [Latest News](/news) [## NAB is modernising data pipelines for its Ada platform](/news/nab-is-modernising-data-pipelines-for-its-ada-platform-627163) [## ANZ Banking Group has a new tech strategy](/news/anz-banking-group-has-a-new-tech-strategy-627175) [## UN chief warns AI outpacing oversight](/news/un-chief-warns-ai-outpacing-oversight-627178) [## FMCG giants give consumer products an AI makeover](/news/fmcg-giants-give-consumer-products-an-ai-makeover-627179) [## Attorney-General's Dept backs Copilot Chat, Google NotebookLM](/news/attorney-generals-dept-backs-copilot-chat-google-notebooklm-627007) * [Home](https://www.itnews.com.au) * [News](https://www.itnews.com.au/news) * [Business](https://www.itnews.com.au/business) * [Financial Services](https://www.itnews.com.au/business/financial-services) # NAB is modernising data pipelines for its Ada platform By [Ry Crozier](/author/ry-crozier-149259) Jul 7 2026 6:41AM ## Backs Spark Declarative Pipelines. NAB is going back and standardising the way data pipelines for its Ada data platform are built, which will lower run costs and make new recovery features available. NAB's Dheeraj Puli. Head of data reliability engineering Dheeraj Puli told the Databricks Data+AI Summit in San Francisco that the bank is standardising on Spark Declarative Pipelines (SDP) across more than 1600 data pipelines. Ada is considered to be “chapter two” of the bank’s data journey, [first breaking cover back in 2022.](https://www.itnews.com.au/news/nab-breaks-cover-with-new-data-platform-ada-587772) It has Databricks on AWS at its core, and [replaced several large data platforms](https://www.itnews.com.au/news/nab-uses-ada-to-shift-to-real-time-data-ingestion-609101), including the NAB Data Hub (NDH) and a [26-year-old Teradata environment.](https://www.itnews.com.au/news/how-nab-unwound-teradatas-tentacles-to-decommission-it-617911) However, there are already opportunities to modernise it. As is common among Databricks customers, NAB has its data in the platform organised or structured according [to a so-called medallion architecture.](https://www.databricks.com/blog/what-is-medallion-architecture) Raw, unaltered data comes in at the bronze layer; it’s then cleaned up in the silver layer ready for use by AI/ML models and the like or curated for more specific business uses at the gold layer. Data typically moves through a multi-hop pipeline as it progresses through the medallion system. Ingestion of raw data to the bronze layer is already standardised, Puli said. “When we started, bronze was actually using SDP from day one,” he said. The opportunity is to modernise the data pipelines used to transform data for the silver and gold layers. These pipelines are still based on Spark but were built in a variety of ways - and it’s this customisation and complexity that the bank wants to now take out of Ada’s operation. SDP represents a different way of building pipelines. Notably, the build is “declarative”, meaning that rather than coding every extract, transform or load (ETL) step, engineers “[define what the data should look like](https://www.databricks.com/customers/national-australian-bank-nab)” and then Databricks - or more specifically, its Lakeflow platform - handles the required “orchestration, state management, and incremental processing” in the background to make it all happen. Work is already well advanced at the silver layer, with new pipelines being built using SDP and older ones being modernised. “All the new silver [pipelines] are going on SDP,” Puli said. “We completed a majority of the uplift of the existing custom Spark SQL [pipelines] to SDP. “Now, we are in the middle of testing those jobs for the safe cutover between custom Spark to the SDP.” A case study published by Databricks to coincide with the US presentation suggests that work at the silver layer is about 50 percent done; at the event itself, Puli said that “currently we have 3800 SDP pipelines running in silver”. The next step will ...
